Real Analysis

Order, Absolute Value, and Completeness of ℝ

Order, Absolute Value, and Completeness of ℝ

Ordering Properties of ℝ

Section 3 of the previous lecture introduced the positive set PPP as the foundation for order on R\mathbb{R}R. We now build out its consequences systematically, moving from the basic order axiomL02s to their arithmetic interactions, and finally to the special behavior of squares, positivity, and products.

Positive Set and Induced Order

Let P⊆RP \subseteq \mathbb{R}P⊆R, P≠∅P \neq \varnothingP=∅, be the set of positive real numbers. Assume:

  1. If a,b∈Pa, b \in Pa,b∈P, then a+b∈Pa + b \in Pa+b∈P.
  2. If a,b∈Pa, b \in Pa,b∈P, then a⋅b∈Pa \cdot b \in Pa⋅b∈P.
  3. Trichotomy: For every a∈Ra \in \mathbb{R}a∈R, exactly one of a∈Pa \in Pa∈P, a=0a = 0a=0, or −a∈P-a \in P−a∈P holds.

Notation:

  • a∈Pa \in Pa∈P means a>0a > 0a>0 (a is positive).
  • −a∈P-a \in P−a∈P means a<0a < 0a<0 (a is negative).
  • a∈P∪{0}a \in P \cup \{0\}a∈P∪{0} means a≥0a \ge 0a≥0 (a is nonnegative).
  • −a∈P∪{0}-a \in P \cup \{0\}−a∈P∪{0} means a≤0a \le 0a≤0 (a is nonpositive).

Induced order:

  • a>ba > ba>b if and only if a−b∈Pa - b \in Pa−b∈P.
  • a≥ba \ge ba≥b if and only if a−b∈P∪{0}a - b \in P \cup \{0\}a−b∈P∪{0}.
  • For any a,b∈Ra, b \in \mathbb{R}a,b∈R, exactly one of a>ba > ba>b, a=ba = ba=b, a<ba < ba<b holds.

Basic Order Properties

Since every order statement reduces to membership in PPP, the two closure properties of PPP translate directly into the two most fundamental facts about >>>: it is transitive, and it is preserved under translation.

Transitivity

If a,b,c∈Ra, b, c \in \mathbb{R}a,b,c∈R with a>ba > ba>b and b>cb > cb>c, then a>ca > ca>c.

Proof

Since a−b∈Pa - b \in Pa−b∈P and b−c∈Pb - c \in Pb−c∈P, Property 1 gives (a−b)+(b−c)=a−c∈P(a-b) + (b-c) = a - c \in P(a−b)+(b−c)=a−c∈P, hence a>ca > ca>c.

Addition Preserves Order

If a>ba > ba>b, then for every c∈Rc \in \mathbb{R}c∈R, a+c>b+ca + c > b + ca+c>b+c.

Proof

a>b  ⟺  a−b∈Pa > b \iff a - b \in Pa>b⟺a−b∈P. Then (a+c)−(b+c)=a−b∈P(a+c) - (b+c) = a - b \in P(a+c)−(b+c)=a−b∈P, so a+c>b+ca + c > b + ca+c>b+c.

Multiplication is more delicate than addition, since multiplying by a negative number reverses an inequality. This asymmetry traces directly back to Property 2 of PPP: multiplying two positives stays positive, but multiplying a positive difference by a negative number lands outside PPP.

Multiplication and Order

Let a,b,c∈Ra, b, c \in \mathbb{R}a,b,c∈R.

  • If a>ba > ba>b and c>0c > 0c>0, then ac>bcac > bcac>bc.
  • If a>ba > ba>b and c<0c < 0c<0, then ac<bcac < bcac<bc.
Proof

If a>ba > ba>b, then a−b∈Pa - b \in Pa−b∈P. If c>0c > 0c>0, then c∈Pc \in Pc∈P, and by Property 2, (a−b)c∈P(a-b)c \in P(a−b)c∈P, i.e. ac−bc∈Pac - bc \in Pac−bc∈P, so ac>bcac > bcac>bc.

If c<0c < 0c<0, then −c∈P-c \in P−c∈P. Since (a−b)(−c)∈P(a-b)(-c) \in P(a−b)(−c)∈P, we have bc−ac∈Pbc - ac \in Pbc−ac∈P, i.e. ac<bcac < bcac<bc.

Squares and Positivity

A first payoff of these order properties is that we can now say something that field axioms alone cannot: squares are never negative. This is a genuinely order-theoretic fact — nothing in the field axioms from Lecture 2 forces it.

Squares Are Nonnegative

If a∈Ra \in \mathbb{R}a∈R, then a2≥0a^2 \ge 0a2≥0, and if a≠0a \neq 0a=0 then a2>0a^2 > 0a2>0.

Proof

By trichotomy, either a∈Pa \in Pa∈P, a=0a = 0a=0, or −a∈P-a \in P−a∈P.

  • If a=0a = 0a=0, then a2=0a^2 = 0a2=0.
  • If a∈Pa \in Pa∈P, then a⋅a∈Pa \cdot a \in Pa⋅a∈P by Property 2, so a2>0a^2 > 0a2>0.
  • If −a∈P-a \in P−a∈P, then (−a)⋅(−a)∈P(-a)\cdot(-a) \in P(−a)⋅(−a)∈P by Property 2, and (−a)(−a)=a2(-a)(-a) = a^2(−a)(−a)=a2, so a2>0a^2 > 0a2>0.

Since 1=121 = 1^21=12 and 1≠01 \neq 01=0, this theorem immediately pins down the sign of 111 — a fact we might otherwise have taken for granted.

1 Is Positive

1>01 > 01>0.

Proof

In R\mathbb{R}R, 1≠01 \neq 01=0. By the Squares theorem, 12>01^2 > 012>0, hence 1>01 > 01>0.

Natural Numbers Are Positive

Every n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N is positive.

Proof

Base case: 1>01 > 01>0. Inductive step: if k>0k > 0k>0, then k,1∈Pk, 1 \in Pk,1∈P, so k+1∈Pk + 1 \in Pk+1∈P by Property 1. By induction, every n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N is positive.

Signs of a Product

Having settled when a single element is positive, we turn to products of two elements — generalizing the sign rule everyone learns informally (“negative times negative is positive”) into a proven theorem.

Sign of a Product

For a,b∈Ra, b \in \mathbb{R}a,b∈R:

  • ab>0ab > 0ab>0 if and only if either (a>0a > 0a>0 and b>0b > 0b>0) or (a<0a < 0a<0 and b<0b < 0b<0).
  • ab<0ab < 0ab<0 if and only if either (a>0a > 0a>0 and b<0b < 0b<0) or (a<0a < 0a<0 and b>0b > 0b>0).
Proof

If a>0a > 0a>0 and b>0b > 0b>0, then ab∈Pab \in Pab∈P by Property 2, so ab>0ab > 0ab>0. If a<0a < 0a<0 and b<0b < 0b<0, then (−a),(−b)∈P(-a), (-b) \in P(−a),(−b)∈P, so (−a)(−b)∈P(-a)(-b) \in P(−a)(−b)∈P; since (−a)(−b)=ab(-a)(-b) = ab(−a)(−b)=ab, we get ab>0ab > 0ab>0.

Conversely, if ab>0ab > 0ab>0 and a>0a > 0a>0, then bbb must be >0> 0>0 — otherwise b≤0b \le 0b≤0 would force ab≤0ab \le 0ab≤0, a contradiction. If ab>0ab > 0ab>0 and a<0a < 0a<0, then b<0b < 0b<0 by the same reasoning.

The second statement follows the same way: if a>0,b<0a > 0, b < 0a>0,b<0 (or vice versa), then ab<0ab < 0ab<0; conversely, if ab<0ab < 0ab<0, the factors must have opposite signs by trichotomy.

Sign table
aaabbbababab
+++
+−−
−+−
−−+

Absolute Values

With order fully in place, we can define a single quantity — the absolute value — that measures magnitude independent of sign. Everything that follows is really about relating ∣⋅∣|\cdot|∣⋅∣ back to the order properties just established.

Absolute Value

For a∈Ra \in \mathbb{R}a∈R, define

∣a∣={a,a>0,0,a=0,−a,a<0.|a| = \begin{cases} a, & a > 0, \\ 0, & a = 0, \\ -a, & a < 0. \end{cases}∣a∣=⎩⎨⎧​a,0,−a,​a>0,a=0,a<0.​

Multiplicative Properties

Product Rule

For all a,b∈Ra, b \in \mathbb{R}a,b∈R, ∣ab∣=∣a∣⋅∣b∣|ab| = |a|\cdot|b|∣ab∣=∣a∣⋅∣b∣.

Proof

Consider cases by the signs of aaa and bbb:

  • If a,b≥0a, b \ge 0a,b≥0: ∣ab∣=ab=∣a∣∣b∣|ab| = ab = |a||b|∣ab∣=ab=∣a∣∣b∣.
  • If a≥0,b≤0a \ge 0, b \le 0a≥0,b≤0: ∣ab∣=−ab=a(−b)=∣a∣∣b∣|ab| = -ab = a(-b) = |a||b|∣ab∣=−ab=a(−b)=∣a∣∣b∣.
  • If a≤0,b≥0a \le 0, b \ge 0a≤0,b≥0: similarly ∣ab∣=(−a)b=∣a∣∣b∣|ab| = (-a)b = |a||b|∣ab∣=(−a)b=∣a∣∣b∣.
  • If a,b≤0a, b \le 0a,b≤0: ∣ab∣=ab=(−a)(−b)=∣a∣∣b∣|ab| = ab = (-a)(-b) = |a||b|∣ab∣=ab=(−a)(−b)=∣a∣∣b∣.

Since a2a^2a2 is a special case of a product (a⋅aa \cdot aa⋅a), the Product Rule immediately tells us how ∣⋅∣|\cdot|∣⋅∣ interacts with squaring.

Square Rule

For all a∈Ra \in \mathbb{R}a∈R, ∣a∣2=a2|a|^2 = a^2∣a∣2=a2.

Proof

By the Product Rule, ∣a∣2=∣a∣∣a∣=∣a2∣|a|^2 = |a||a| = |a^2|∣a∣2=∣a∣∣a∣=∣a2∣. Since a2≥0a^2 \ge 0a2≥0 (by the Squares theorem above), ∣a2∣=a2|a^2| = a^2∣a2∣=a2.

Order Properties and the Triangle Inequality

The next result is the key bridge between absolute value and order: it converts a single statement "∣a∣≤c|a| \le c∣a∣≤c" into a two-sided inequality, which is exactly the form needed to add inequalities together — the technique the triangle inequality relies on below.

Order Bound Characterization

If c≥0c \ge 0c≥0, then ∣a∣≤c|a| \le c∣a∣≤c if and only if −c≤a≤c-c \le a \le c−c≤a≤c.

Proof

(⇒\Rightarrow⇒) If ∣a∣≤c|a| \le c∣a∣≤c, then −∣a∣≤a≤∣a∣-|a| \le a \le |a|−∣a∣≤a≤∣a∣ implies −c≤a≤c-c \le a \le c−c≤a≤c.

(⇐\Leftarrow⇐) If −c≤a≤c-c \le a \le c−c≤a≤c, then checking cases on the sign of aaa directly gives ∣a∣≤c|a| \le c∣a∣≤c.

Basic Bound

For all a∈Ra \in \mathbb{R}a∈R, −∣a∣≤a≤∣a∣-|a| \le a \le |a|−∣a∣≤a≤∣a∣.

Proof

This is the previous theorem with c=∣a∣c = |a|c=∣a∣.

We now have exactly the tool needed to prove the single most important inequality in analysis: the triangle inequality. The strategy is to apply the Basic Bound to aaa and to bbb separately, then add the two two-sided inequalities.

Triangle Inequality

For all a,b∈Ra, b \in \mathbb{R}a,b∈R, ∣a+b∣≤∣a∣+∣b∣|a + b| \le |a| + |b|∣a+b∣≤∣a∣+∣b∣.

Proof

From −∣a∣≤a≤∣a∣-|a| \le a \le |a|−∣a∣≤a≤∣a∣ and −∣b∣≤b≤∣b∣-|b| \le b \le |b|−∣b∣≤b≤∣b∣, add componentwise:

−(∣a∣+∣b∣)≤a+b≤∣a∣+∣b∣.-(|a|+|b|) \le a+b \le |a|+|b|.−(∣a∣+∣b∣)≤a+b≤∣a∣+∣b∣.

By the Order Bound Characterization, this gives ∣a+b∣≤∣a∣+∣b∣|a+b| \le |a|+|b|∣a+b∣≤∣a∣+∣b∣.

Two Useful Consequences

For all a,b∈Ra, b \in \mathbb{R}a,b∈R:

  1. ∣a−b∣≤∣a∣+∣b∣|a-b| \le |a| + |b|∣a−b∣≤∣a∣+∣b∣.
  2. ∣∣a∣−∣b∣∣≤∣a−b∣\big||a| - |b|\big| \le |a - b|​∣a∣−∣b∣​≤∣a−b∣.
Proof

(1) Apply the triangle inequality to a+(−b)a + (-b)a+(−b):

∣a−b∣=∣a+(−b)∣≤∣a∣+∣−b∣=∣a∣+∣b∣.|a-b| = |a + (-b)| \le |a| + |{-b}| = |a| + |b|.∣a−b∣=∣a+(−b)∣≤∣a∣+∣−b∣=∣a∣+∣b∣.

(2) Apply the triangle inequality to (a−b)+b(a-b) + b(a−b)+b:

∣a∣=∣(a−b)+b∣≤∣a−b∣+∣b∣  ⟹  ∣a∣−∣b∣≤∣a−b∣.|a| = |(a-b)+b| \le |a-b| + |b| \implies |a| - |b| \le |a-b|.∣a∣=∣(a−b)+b∣≤∣a−b∣+∣b∣⟹∣a∣−∣b∣≤∣a−b∣.

Swapping aaa and bbb gives ∣b∣−∣a∣≤∣a−b∣|b| - |a| \le |a-b|∣b∣−∣a∣≤∣a−b∣. Combining the two,

∣∣a∣−∣b∣∣≤∣a−b∣.\big||a|-|b|\big| \le |a-b|.​∣a∣−∣b∣​≤∣a−b∣.
The pattern behind both consequences

Both parts reduce to a single application of the triangle inequality to a cleverly rewritten sum (a+(−b)a + (-b)a+(−b), or (a−b)+b(a-b)+b(a−b)+b). This is a recurring proof technique: many “variant” inequalities are not new results at all, but the triangle inequality applied to a disguised sum.

Completeness of ℝ

Everything so far — field axioms, order, absolute value — is also satisfied by Q\mathbb{Q}Q. Yet we proved earlier that [[Irrationality of √2|2∉Q\sqrt{2} \notin \mathbb{Q}2​∈/Q]], so Q\mathbb{Q}Q has “gaps” that R\mathbb{R}R does not. The property that finally distinguishes R\mathbb{R}R from Q\mathbb{Q}Q is completeness, which we develop now.

Upper and Lower Bounds; Bounded Sets

Let S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R be nonempty.

  • SSS is bounded above if there exists u∈Ru \in \mathbb{R}u∈R such that s≤us \le us≤u for all s∈Ss \in Ss∈S. Any such uuu is an upper bound of SSS.
  • SSS is bounded below if there exists w∈Rw \in \mathbb{R}w∈R such that s≥ws \ge ws≥w for all s∈Ss \in Ss∈S. Any such www is a lower bound of SSS.
  • SSS is bounded if it has both an upper and a lower bound; otherwise it is unbounded.

An upper bound need not be unique — if uuu works, so does u+1u+1u+1. This motivates asking for the best possible bound.

Supremum and Infimum

Let S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R be nonempty.

  • uuu is the supremum (least upper bound) of SSS, written u=sup⁡Su = \sup Su=supS, if:
    1. uuu is an upper bound of SSS, and
    2. for every upper bound vvv of SSS, u≤vu \le vu≤v.
  • www is the infimum (greatest lower bound) of SSS, written w=inf⁡Sw = \inf Sw=infS, if:
    1. www is a lower bound of SSS, and
    2. for every lower bound ttt of SSS, t≤wt \le wt≤w.

The definition of supremum only tells us what it means to be a least upper bound — it says nothing about whether one always exists. That existence is not provable from the field and order axioms alone; it must be taken as an additional axiom, and it is precisely this axiom that Q\mathbb{Q}Q fails to satisfy (e.g. S={r∈Q:r2<2}S = \{r \in \mathbb{Q} : r^2 < 2\}S={r∈Q:r2<2} has no rational supremum).

Least Upper Bound Property (Completeness)

Every nonempty subset S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R that is bounded above has a supremum in R\mathbb{R}R.

Dually, every nonempty subset S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R that is bounded below has an infimum in R\mathbb{R}R.

In practice, verifying that a specific uuu equals sup⁡S\sup SsupS directly from the definition can be awkward, since condition 2 quantifies over all upper bounds. The following characterization replaces that quantifier with a more workable ε\varepsilonε-statement.

Characterization of the Supremum

Let S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R be nonempty and u∈Ru \in \mathbb{R}u∈R. Then u=sup⁡Su = \sup Su=supS if and only if:

  1. For all s∈Ss \in Ss∈S, s≤us \le us≤u.
  2. For every ε>0\varepsilon > 0ε>0, there exists sε∈Ss_\varepsilon \in Ssε​∈S such that u−ε<sεu - \varepsilon < s_\varepsilonu−ε<sε​.
Reading condition 2

Condition 2 says exactly that no number smaller than uuu can be an upper bound of SSS: for any proposed “smaller candidate” u−εu - \varepsilonu−ε, some element of SSS slips past it. This is the practical, checkable substitute for ”u≤vu \le vu≤v for every upper bound vvv” in the original definition, and it is usually how sup⁡S=u\sup S = usupS=u is verified in practice.
